Name: spectral_latent_projection
Title: Spectral Latent Projection: A Training-Free Framework for Latency-Aware Inference Optimization
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by projecting activations onto a dynamically identified low-rank subspace during inference, we can minimize perturbation overhead and maintain strict latency budgets without requiring retraining or extensive pre-processing.
Related Work: Existing works like Spectral Quantization Routing and Spectral Latent Steering explore optimization at inference but often involve static methods or opaque heuristics. Our proposal distinguishes itself by employing a dynamic, training-free projection mechanism that directly addresses latent perturbation and enforces strict latency constraints, leveraging spectral properties of activations for real-time inference.
Abstract: We introduce Spectral Latent Projection (SLP), a novel training-free framework designed to optimize inference efficiency while adhering to strict latency budgets. The core idea is to dynamically project model activations onto a low-rank subspace identified through a single-pass PCA at the beginning of inference. This approach not only minimizes hidden-state perturbation but also ensures that the geometric structure of the optimization landscape is preserved. By leveraging spectral properties, SLP can adaptively maintain inference performance across varying input distributions, thereby enhancing model robustness in real-world applications. Our empirical evaluations will focus on latency, throughput, and accuracy under strict computational constraints, demonstrating that SLP achieves a favorable throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to existing methods. We will provide a theoretical grounding linking spectral properties to optimization stability, along with rigorous empirical validation to ensure replication and transparency in our approach.
Experiments: 1. Implement the SLP framework as a module within existing architectures (e.g., transformers, CNNs) and evaluate its performance on standard datasets (e.g., ImageNet, GLUE).
2. Compare SLP against baseline models that utilize full-rank activations and existing training-free methods under controlled latency budgets.
3. Measure and analyze the latency, throughput, and accuracy, focusing on how well SLP maintains performance across varying latent dimensions and input types.
4. Use ablation studies to isolate the effects of the low-rank projection on hidden-state perturbation and overall model robustness.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential risks include the challenge of accurately identifying an optimal low-dimensional subspace that generalizes well across diverse inputs. Additionally, the method's effectiveness may vary depending on the architecture and task, necessitating careful evaluation. Limitations may arise in extreme cases of data distribution shifts, where the projected subspace may not capture essential features.
